content:
# Numbers Chapter 13 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 162-164 | Codex Vaticanus pa_0121-0122
**Source Images:** `NUM_013_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_013_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_013_page_003.jpg`

---

**1** YHWH spoke to Moses and said,

**2** "Send out men to scout the land of Canaan, which I am giving to the Sons of Israel. Send one man from each ancestral tribe, each one a leader among them."

**3** So Moses sent them out from the wilderness of Paran at the command of YHWH. All of them were leaders of the Sons of Israel.

**4** These were their names: from the tribe of Reuben, Shammua son of Zaccur;

**5** from the tribe of Simeon, Shaphat son of Hori;

**6** from the tribe of Judah, Caleb son of Jephunneh;

**7** from the tribe of Issachar, Igal son of Joseph;

**8** from the tribe of Ephraim, Hoshea son of Nun;

**9** from the tribe of Benjamin, Palti son of Raphu;

**10** from the tribe of Zebulun, Gaddiel son of Sodi;

**11** from the tribe of Joseph -- that is, the tribe of Manasseh -- Gaddi son of Susi;

**12** from the tribe of Dan, Ammiel son of Gemalli;

**13** from the tribe of Asher, Sethur son of Michael;

**14** from the tribe of Naphtali, Nahbi son of Vophsi;

**15** from the tribe of Gad, Geuel son of Machi.

**16** These are the names of the men Moses sent to scout the land. And Moses gave Hoshea son of Nun the name Joshua.

**17** When Moses sent them to scout the land of Canaan, he told them, "Go up through the Negev, and then continue into the hill country.

**18** See what the land is like. Are the people who live there strong or weak? Are they few or many?

**19** Is the land they inhabit good or bad? Are the towns where they live open settlements or fortified cities?

**20** Is the soil rich or poor? Are there trees in it or not? Be bold, and bring back some of the fruit of the land." It was the season when the first grapes ripen.

**21** So they went up and scouted the land from the wilderness of Zin all the way to Rehob, near Lebo-hamath.

**22** They went up through the Negev and came to Hebron, where Ahiman, Sheshai, and Talmai -- descendants of Anak -- were living. (Hebron had been built seven years before Zoan in Egypt.)

**23** When they reached the Wadi Eshcol, they cut down a branch with a single cluster of grapes so heavy that two men had to carry it on a pole between them. They also took some pomegranates and figs.

**24** That place was called the Wadi Eshcol -- meaning "Cluster Valley" -- because of the cluster of grapes the Sons of Israel cut there.

**25** After forty days, they returned from scouting the land.

**26** They went back to Moses, Aaron, and the entire community of the Sons of Israel at Kadesh in the wilderness of Paran. They brought their report to the whole assembly and showed them the fruit of the land.

**27** They told Moses, "We went into the land where you sent us. It truly does flow with milk and honey -- here is its fruit!

**28** However, the people who live in the land are powerful, and the cities are fortified and very large. We even saw descendants of Anak there.

**29** The Amalekites occupy the Negev; the Hittites, Jebusites, and Amorites live in the hill country; and the Canaanites live along the sea and beside the Jordan."

**30** Then Caleb silenced the people who were grumbling against Moses and said, "We should go up at once and take possession of the land -- we are more than able to do it!"

**31** But the men who had gone up with him said, "We cannot attack those people -- they are too strong for us."

**32** So they spread a demoralizing report among the Sons of Israel about the land they had scouted. They said, "The land we explored devours those who settle in it, and all the people we saw there are men of great stature.

**33** We even saw the Nephilim there -- the descendants of Anak come from the Nephilim! In our own eyes we seemed like grasshoppers, and that is how we must have looked to them."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 2 -- "Send out men to scout" (שְׁלַח־לְךָ אֲנָשִׁים וְיָתֻרוּ / shelach-lekha anashim veyaturu):** The Hebrew *shelach-lekha* ("send for yourself") contains a reflexive nuance -- YHWH is not commanding the reconnaissance mission but authorizing it at Moses' initiative. The *lekha* ("for yourself") suggests this is Moses' undertaking, endorsed by YHWH but not originating from him. This becomes theologically significant in Deuteronomy 1:22-23, where Moses recounts that the idea came from the people themselves. The verb *tur* (תור) means to explore, reconnoiter, investigate -- it carries a sense of careful examination, not mere traveling through. The LXX Vaticanus uses ἀποστειλόν ("send away/dispatch") and κατασκέψασθαι ("to spy out"), which intensifies the military dimension. I rendered "scout" rather than "spy" because the Hebrew term emphasizes exploration over espionage, though the mission clearly has military intelligence purposes.

**Verse 3 -- "all of them were leaders" (כֻּלָּם אֲנָשִׁים רָאשֵׁי בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל / kullam anashim rashei venei-yisra'el):** The word *rashim* ("heads") indicates these were not ordinary scouts but tribal chieftains -- men of standing and authority whose testimony would carry weight with the entire assembly. This detail matters for the disaster that follows: when leaders deliver a faithless report, the damage is catastrophic because the people trust their judgment. The narrative carefully establishes their credentials before recording their failure.

**Verse 16 -- "Moses gave Hoshea the name Joshua" (וַיִּקְרָא מֹשֶׁה לְהוֹשֵׁעַ בִּן־נוּן יְהוֹשֻׁעַ):** The original name *Hoshea* (הוֹשֵׁעַ) means "he saves" or "salvation." Moses changes it to *Yehoshua* (יְהוֹשֻׁעַ), which prefixes the divine name -- "YHWH saves." The renaming is theologically loaded: it shifts the agency of salvation from the human ("he saves") to the divine ("YHWH is the one who saves"). This occurs precisely at the moment Joshua is being sent into a situation where human strength will prove insufficient and divine faithfulness will be the decisive factor. The timing of the name-change in the narrative -- mentioned right after the roster of twelve scouts -- singles Joshua out before the events unfold, foreshadowing that he alone (with Caleb) will prove faithful.

**Verse 20 -- "the season when the first grapes ripen" (וְהַיָּמִים יְמֵי בִּכּוּרֵי עֲנָבִים / vehayamim yemei bikkurei anavim):** This temporal note places the scouting mission in late summer (roughly July-August), when the first grape clusters ripen in the hill country of Canaan. The detail serves a narrative purpose: it explains why the scouts were able to bring back the spectacular grape cluster of Eshcol. It also frames the land's bounty as a tangible, seasonal reality -- the promise is not abstract but edible. The word *bikkurim* ("firstfruits") carries cultic overtones, connecting the land's produce to Israel's worship obligations.

**Verse 22 -- "descendants of Anak" (יְלִידֵי הָעֲנָק / yelidei ha'anak):** The *Anakim* were legendary inhabitants of Canaan renowned for their extraordinary height and military power. The name *Anak* (עֲנָק) means "long-necked" or "giant," and their mention here is loaded with intimidation. The three named individuals -- Ahiman, Sheshai, and Talmai -- are specified to make the threat concrete and personal: these are not vague legends but particular warriors holding particular territory. The parenthetical about Hebron being built seven years before Zoan (Tanis, the Egyptian royal city) anchors the narrative in historical geography, establishing Hebron's antiquity and importance. The LXX Vaticanus preserves the Anak reference as γίγαντες ("giants").

**Verse 23 -- "a single cluster of grapes so heavy that two men had to carry it" (וַיִּשָּׂאֻהוּ בַמּוֹט בִּשְׁנָיִם):** The Hebrew *mot* (מוֹט) is a carrying pole -- the same word used for the poles that bore the Ark of the Covenant and the tabernacle furnishings. The image of two men hauling a single grape cluster on a sacred-type pole is deliberately evocative: the fruit of the promised land is being transported like a holy object. This cluster becomes the physical evidence that the land is everything YHWH promised -- and makes the subsequent rejection all the more damning. The scouts have seen the proof and will still refuse to trust. The name *Eshcol* (אֶשְׁכּוֹל) means "cluster," and the etiological note in verse 24 explains the place-name by the event, a common Hebrew narrative device.

**Verse 27 -- "It truly does flow with milk and honey" (זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבַשׁ / zavat chalav ud'vash):** Even the faithless scouts cannot deny the land's abundance. The participle *zavat* ("flowing") suggests continuous, effortless overflowing -- the land does not merely produce milk and honey but gushes with them. *Chalav* (milk) represents pastoral wealth; *d'vash* (honey -- probably date syrup or wild bee honey) represents agricultural sweetness. Together they are a merism for total abundance, a land that sustains both herder and farmer. The phrase echoes YHWH's original promise to Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:8, 17). That the scouts confirm the promise while simultaneously arguing against trusting it is the chapter's central irony.

**Verse 30 -- "Caleb silenced the people" (וַיַּהַס כָּלֵב אֶת־הָעָם / vayyahas Kalev et-ha'am):** The verb *has* (הַס) means to hush, to silence -- it is an authoritative act, not a gentle request. Caleb does not merely offer an alternative opinion; he commands the crowd to be quiet. His declaration עָלֹה נַעֲלֶה ("going up, we shall go up") uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis -- it expresses absolute certainty. The phrase יָכוֹל נוּכַל לָהּ ("we are certainly able to do it") mirrors the same emphatic construction. Caleb's confidence is not in Israel's military strength but in YHWH's promise. His name (כָּלֵב) is sometimes connected to *kelev* ("dog" -- i.e., wholly devoted, following at the master's heel), and his character here lives up to the wordplay: he follows YHWH without reservation.

**Verse 32 -- "a demoralizing report" (דִּבַּת הָאָרֶץ / dibbat ha'aretz):** The word *dibbah* (דִּבָּה) means slander, defamation, an evil report -- it is not merely a negative assessment but a deliberate distortion. The same word appears in Proverbs 10:18 and Genesis 37:2 (Joseph bringing a "bad report" about his brothers). The scouts are not simply pessimistic; they are bearing false witness against the land YHWH promised. The phrase "the land devours those who settle in it" (אֶרֶץ אֹכֶלֶת יוֹשְׁבֶיהָ) is hyperbolic slander -- the scouts portray the promised land as a death trap, contradicting the milk-and-honey testimony they just gave. This contradiction exposes the report as driven by fear rather than fact. The LXX renders dibbah as ἔκστασιν ("panic/astonishment"), capturing the emotional devastation the report caused.

**Verse 33 -- "the Nephilim" (הַנְּפִילִים / hannefilim) and "like grasshoppers" (כַּחֲגָבִים / kachagavim):** The Nephilim are mentioned only here and in Genesis 6:4, where they appear as the mighty beings of the pre-flood world. The scouts' claim that Anak's descendants are Nephilim is likely exaggeration meant to terrify -- they are importing primordial mythology into their intelligence report. The grasshopper simile is devastating in its self-assessment: the scouts have already defeated themselves in their own imagination. The Hebrew is psychologically precise: וַנְּהִי בְעֵינֵינוּ כַּחֲגָבִים ("we became like grasshoppers in our own eyes") -- the diminishment begins internally, as a failure of self-perception, before being projected onto the enemy: וְכֵן הָיִינוּ בְּעֵינֵיהֶם ("and so we were in their eyes"). The tragedy of this verse is that Israel's defeat is not military but spiritual: they have measured themselves against giants instead of measuring the giants against YHWH.
